club.name: Daylight Computer Club
club.tagline: Homebrew apps for your Daylight, shared between friends.

apps.name: Daylight Keys
apps.tagline: Volume keys become brightness keys, a snip screenshot tool, and the dog-ear corner.
apps.author: Anjan
apps.description: Press Vol Up + Vol Down together to toggle the volume keys between controlling volume and screen brightness — with fine single-unit steps at the dim end for nighttime reading, and a tap-the-sun backlight-off. Long-press the quick action button for a drag-a-rectangle screenshot snip. A folded dog-ear corner in the top-left reveals the time, your pomodoro, and your next event as you pull it.
inspected: permissions read straight from the app file
built from open source you can read
afterInstall: Open the Daylight Keys app from your home screen.
Turn on its accessibility service: open Settings, then Accessibility, find “dc1-keys”, and switch it on. (This is what lets it listen to the volume keys.)
Allow “Modify system settings” if the app asks — that's how it changes brightness.
Say yes to the notification, microphone, and calendar prompts if you want the dog-ear's timer and next-event features.

apps.name: The Website
apps.tagline: The same club through any window — pin it on any device, or just keep the link.
apps.author: Anjan
apps.description: The club as a web page — daylightcomputer.club. It works in any browser on any device (iPhone, Mac, a friend's laptop), and on Android you can pin it to your home screen where it opens full-screen like an app. On a Daylight, the Clubhouse app is the cozier door — this is the door that's always open everywhere else.

apps.name: The Clubhouse
apps.tagline: The club, at home on your Daylight — the shelf inside, gifts at the door, one-tap installs.
apps.author: Anjan
apps.description: The club's home on your tablet. The shelf lives inside it, and it does what only a real app can: a few times a day it glances at the club — a friend brought a dish → a quiet knock; a dish got better → an update note; a dish was pulled → a recall note with exactly what to do. Installing becomes one tap — it fetches the dish and hands it to Android's installer (Android still asks you to confirm; nothing ever installs silently). Its house inspector explains in plain words when two apps might fight over your keys or draw over each other, with a button to the exact Settings page. And if a dish misbehaves, one tap builds a report you can text to whoever made it — written so their Claude can read it and fix the dish. Everything it sees stays on your tablet.
permissions: ask Android to install apps (you confirm each one)
see what apps are installed (to spot collisions — never leaves the tablet)
inspected: permissions read straight from the app file
built from open source you can read
afterInstall: Open The Clubhouse from your home screen.
Allow notifications when it asks — that's how it tells you a friend brought a dish.
The first time you use a one-tap install, Android asks once to let The Clubhouse install apps: flip the switch and come back. Every install after that is one tap plus Android's confirm.
